---
title: "External Triggers"
format: html
---
# External Triggers

External triggers are people, places, objects, and situations that become linked with alcohol use over time. In simple terms, this means your brain has learned to connect certain cues with drinking. Even after you decide to stop drinking, your brain can still react quickly when it sees old cues (for example, a favorite bar, a certain friend group, payday, or a route home that passes a liquor store). That fast reaction is not a character flaw; it is a learned habit loop.

The good news is that external triggers can be managed. Research shows that risk drops when you identify cues early, reduce unnecessary exposure, and use a specific plan before urges escalate. Planning works better than relying on willpower in the moment.

External triggers are highest risk when they are unexpected and combined with low structure. Planning your response in advance makes decision-making easier in the moment and makes it easier to follow through under pressure.

## Trigger Mapping Worksheet
| External trigger | Where/when it happens | Risk (0-10) | My prevention step |
|---|---|---:|---|
|  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |

::: {.callout-tip}
### Skill rehearsal

Treat this as a rehearsal for moments when motivation is low and pressure is high.

1. Choose your top two high-risk external triggers.
2. Write one **avoidance plan** and one **exit plan** for each.
3. Practice your plan this week and record what helped.

Repetition builds confidence and makes healthy responses easier to access.
:::
